titleEn: Spare Your Tears for My Resolve

verses.sadr: فتى النكبات من يأوي إذا ما
فما في الأرض من شرف يفاع

verses.ajuz: على ما فيك من كرم الطباع

translationEn: Spare the tears of your eye from my resolve, and guard whatever veil you have removed. Enough — your weeping has straitened my patience, though no calamity has straitened my forearm. O you who are accustomed to lamentation — how many separations have I endured that became the cause of reunion! The joy of homecoming belongs only to one bound to the grief of farewell. She grieved to see my body grown thin, as though glory were won by wrestling. The youth of misfortunes — when they settle upon him, he turns to character both vast and ample. He raises clouds of dust at every frontier, mad with the spirit of ʿAdiyy ibn al-Ruqāʿ. Dwell with the lions of the wilderness until the lions mistake him for one of their own kin. Then clothe yourself in resolution if you ever attempt to accomplish the impossible. You did not journey like the swift she-camel on the road, nor did you ride your anxieties like a man of firm purpose. Through Mahdī ibn Aṣram my branch returned to its leafing and my reach extended wide. He lengthened my arm against the days until I repaid fortune's turns measure for measure. When the herds of poetry found no pasture, his gifts became their grazing grounds. Gardens from which fragrance never strays and which are never empty of lofty ambitions. He strove and drew down honor through power — without striving there would be no strivings. O Mahdī, I greet you on account of your gift — how much blaming sounds to one who does not heed! You intended, where lofty deeds are never disobeyed, that generosity be disobeyed and that you be obeyed. Pillar of rescue when the turns of nights assault and their champion confronts confrontation. Often the high lances fill him with longing and his ambition aspires to precious possessions. It is as though, on the morning of alarm, he had drunk wine — described to him as the soul of the brave. A beautiful death in nobility and piety is dearer to him than a beautiful defense. And the voice of a petitioner hoping in him is sweeter to his ears than melodious song. You made generosity the luster of your deeds — can a sun exist without its rays? There is nothing on earth more resistant to restraint that drives blame away than an obeyed generosity. Nothing preserves lost glory like lost wealth. May God protect you for the sake of kindness — I see you as no guardian of your wealth's pasture. There is no lofty honor on earth that you have not preceded, nor a lofty character. Your resolve is like the resolve of a flood strengthened by its channels and watercourses. Your judgment is like the judgment of a sword whose edge is sound in counsel at the clash. If you were to paint your own portrait you could not add to what is already in you of noble character.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my firm resolve, my determined intention
weeping, sobbing, lamentation
homecomings, returns from journeys
dust cloud raised in battle or swift movement
firm resolution, determined purpose
the clash of battle, the moment of confrontation
watercourses, channels that carry floodwater
strivings, noble endeavors, efforts toward glory

context: A panegyric to Mahdī ibn Aṣram in the wāfir meter, opening with an address to a beloved lamenting his departure. Abu Tammam weaves together the nasīb of departure with praise for his patron's generosity and martial resolve, celebrating him as a pillar of rescue and a man whose ambitions surpass all others.

titleAr: بيت قلبي من هواك على الطوى
titleEn: My Heart's House Starves for Your Love

verses.sadr: بيت قلبي من هواك على الطوى
لو لم يجرني الهجر منك بلطفه
لم ترع لي حرقا بقلبي قد مضت
verses.ajuz: لو لم يذدها الدمع عنه لاشتوى
في غفلة إن الهوى ينسي الهوى

translationEn: The house of my heart starves through loving you, and I have departed from the land of yearning and passion. Had separation not restrained me from you through its gentle hand, by God I would have sought refuge from you in remoteness. You showed no care for the burning in my heart that has passed — had tears not driven it away, it would have consumed me. Far from it — I was in the rashness and recklessness of youth, in a heedlessness, for love makes one forget love.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hunger, starvation; also the state of enduring hardship
intense longing, passionate attachment
inner anguish, burning love-grief
separation, abandonment by the beloved
distance, separation, remoteness
youth, the state of being young and inexperienced

context: A brief ghazal in the kāmil meter reflecting on the poet's youthful heedlessness in love and the paradox that separation itself becomes a kind of refuge from the consuming fire of passion.

titleEn: May My Soul Ransom This Beloved

verses.sadr: جحدت الهوى ان كنت مذ جعل الهوى

verses.ajuz: من الشوق إلا أن عيني في عرس

translationEn: May my soul ransom a beloved who will bereave me of my very soul and make my body a gift to the grave and the tomb. I deny passion — if since passion made his beauty my sun I have looked upon the actual sun. The whole world has grown narrow upon me through his abandonment, until I feel as though I am in a prison. I dwell in a heart wandering — within it a mourning assembly of longing — except that my eyes are in a wedding feast.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bereaves me, causes me to lose (as a parent loses a child)
the grave, the burial mound
abandonment, the beloved's long avoidance
a gathering of mourning, a funeral assembly
a wedding celebration, a joyous feast

context: A short ghazal contrasting the desolation of the heart with the brightness of the eyes, built around the paradox of a heart in mourning while the eyes rejoice. The poem is in the ṭawīl meter.

titleAr: قد كسانا من كسوة الصيف خرق
titleEn: A Nobleman Clothed Us in Summer Robes

verses.sadr: قد كسانا من كسوة الصيف خرق
يطرد اليوم ذا الهجير ولو شب
خلعة من أغر أروع رحب الص

translationEn: A generous man of great deeds clothed us in summer garments, himself dressed in noble deeds and strivings. A fine garment and a mantle like the sheen of summer heat, or like the mantle of the brave. Like shimmering mirage in description, except it does not deceive like the mirage. Soft and light as a reed-stalk, the wind sets its back trembling at the command of the blowing breeze. It trembles as if it were, from fate's perspective, the liver of the lovelorn or the bowels of the alarmed. Clinging close to what it wraps, you would think it part of the wearer's loins and ribs. It drives away the fierce midday heat, even if the burning of a farewell day were kindled within it. A robe of honor from a radiant, awe-inspiring man of broad chest, broad heart, and broad reach. I will clothe you in return in praise that covers it — like the craftsman's woven mantle. The beauty of that robe is in the eyes; but this praise — its beauty is in hearts and ears.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a fine, thin fabric of excellent quality
the shimmering mirage, the quivering haze of heat
the intense midday heat of summer
a robe of honor, a gift garment from a patron
the awe-inspiring, the one who fills hearts with wonder
praise, eulogy, commendation

context: A poem of thanks for a summer robe (khilʿa) gifted to the poet, in the khafīf meter. Abu Tammam describes the garment in elaborate detail before pledging to repay the gift with poetic praise that will outlast the robe itself.

titleEn: I Spent the Night at War with Sleep
translationEn: I spent the night at peace with anguish and at war with slumber, exposed to sighing and breathing. All through my night I clasped my liver in my hand, sliced through as if by razors. When griefs descended I groaned and called out: O Abū al-ʿAbbās! My grief from you — may not even a tenth of what has passed over my head from loving you ever touch you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: inner anguish, burning grief of love
slumber, drowsiness, sleep
deep sighing, the exhaling groan of distress
razors, sharp blades used for cutting
one tenth, a fraction

context: A short ghazal of sleepless longing addressed to a beloved called Abū al-ʿAbbās. In the khafīf meter, it captures an intimate night scene of physical anguish ending with a tender wish that the beloved be spared even a fraction of the poet's suffering.

titleEn: Abu Ali and His Pasturing Ground

verses.ajuz: من صاب قول يدمي ومن سلعه
من قدعه إن أمنت من قدعه

verses.sadr: ينزل في الكاهل المنيف من الأم
verses.ajuz: ر وهم تحت ذاك من زمعه

verses.sadr: قد ذاب لي في يديك ذوب السنا
verses.ajuz: م الجعد حكمت الرضف في قمعه

verses.sadr: ولم تغير وجهي عن الصبغة ال
لا بل هنيء الندى هنيء السدى

verses.ajuz: م المجد مجد الرياش في شنعه

verses.sadr: لا في ريام ولا قراه ولا
ساحر نظم سحر البياض من ال

verses.ajuz: ما شئت من تمه ومن قطعه

translationEn: Abū ʿAlī — his name matches his pasturing ground: settle at the upper part of his valley or its sandy hollow. Draw near in imagination and sense from his sight at one moment and his hearing at another. A relentless envier — I said to him: who is wounded by bitter speech and by cactus-thorns? Do not let the black serpents cut off your honor, and pretend not to notice with a conspicuous nose for the one who instigates harm. Let not your two neck-veins be startled by the slightest rebuke if you feel safe from rebuke. Beware of the lion-den — do not circle near it, for I fear for you its beast. You see the great man veiled — yet he has a retinue — and you meet the followed one from among his followers. He settles in the high shoulder-blade of command while they are beneath that in their haste and anxiety. O Lord, many a day whose dawn blazes with the radiant morning light of his generosity. In your hands my coagulated lock melted away — as the heated stones ruled over its suppression. You did not change my face from the hue most fitting for one whose color is scorched and gleaming. Nay rather, congratulations on the generosity, congratulations on the gift — one who hopes in you has not been soiled in his seeking. The messenger came to me with a splendid garment for a man's summer and his spring abode. Whoever finds fault with the strange robe of honor — glory lies in the splendor of fine garments in its strangeness. Had it been draped over Uways it would have hastened pride into his piety. Fine silk pleasant to the touch — the pouring of youthful hands upon one who wears a garment. And a secret brocade as if my poetry brought to life the genealogy of eyes from its novelty. As if the fresh flowers and clotted blood from its clinging and its yielding. And the light of the ʿarār flower running through its embroidery displayed upon its ripeness. Neither in Riyām nor in its villages nor in Zubayda its like, nor in Rumʿa. No eye of any fair-minded person passes over it without blessing its workmanship. You have left me with uplifted eyelids in a pitiless age, proud of its beauty. Habitually haughty and soaring above its festivals, towering over its gatherings. An envier of your gift — I told him, and many a crooked rib have I straightened with a word: You have described a sword but ignored its hilt, and a gazelle resting and forgot its neck. You are our brother and a great king — we bestow the extra of what we receive from him in robes of honor. So wear upon yourself its like, befitting one like you from the ample flowing garment of poetry in its expanse. Arduous are the rhymes except for their rider — the weaver of prosody that resists the incompetent. A sorcerer of arrangement who bewitches whiteness from the free colors — his enchanting deception. A garment of affection that became, before all others, his pasturing ground — we do not say from whose pasture. You preceded until you cut off before them what you wished of its fullness and its extent. And poetry is a path whose particular quality throughout the long nights belongs only to its first opener.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a pasturing ground sought for fresh grazing; figuratively a place of bounty
black serpents; figuratively dangerous, treacherous enemies
a lion's den, a thicket where predators hide
the upper shoulder-blade area; figuratively the high rank of command
a robe of honor granted by a patron as a gift
poetry, verse; the craft of measured composition
its first opener, the one who first breaks open a difficult path

context: A poem in the munṣarih meter thanking Abū ʿAlī for a splendid garment, containing a long description of the silk robe and concluding with Abu Tammam's assertion of his poetic mastery. He also addresses an envier, warning against jealousy and praising poetry as a domain conquered only by its true master.

titleEn: Distance Separated Him from His Kin
translationEn: Distance has removed him from his relatives, so the rope was cast over the back of the camel — he was set free to wander. His beloved lived on with her resistance; the hope of his seeker died upon that resistance. Beauty united in him while the paths of reason diverged in his various ways. I have never seen a full moon like you, so perfectly balanced that it needs no stars around it. How admirable is the passionate lover who hurled himself at your initial hardness until it softened with his own gentle persistence. I met you when your beginning was a wonder, and I did not think of the consequences. And one who is truly good — no wonder that people partake of his best qualities.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the hump of a camel; to cast a rope over it means to set free
resistance, the act of withholding and refusing
the paths or directions of reason and understanding
perfectly balanced, harmonious in all aspects
the finest and best qualities; sweet things

context: A short ghazal in the munṣarih meter praising an idealized beloved's beauty. The poem ends with a witty proverb-like verse: that from a truly good person it is natural for all to benefit.

titleAr: غدا يتناءى صاحب كان لي انسا
titleEn: Tomorrow a Dear Companion Parts

verses.sadr: غدا يتناءى صاحب كان لي انسا
verses.ajuz: فلا مصبح لي في السرور ولا ممسا

verses.sadr: أخ لي لو أعطى المنى باسم فقده
verses.ajuz: بلا فقده كانت به ثمنا بخسا

verses.sadr: فلو أن نفسي الف نفس لما انثنت
verses.ajuz: يد البين أو تودي بآخرها نفسا

translationEn: Tomorrow a companion who was my intimate draws far away, so I have neither morning nor evening left in joy. My sorrows over him will grow many, and my good fortune from his affection turns to ill omen. A brother to me — had the wishes been granted in exchange for losing him, without actually losing him, the price would be a pittance. Were my soul a thousand souls, the hand of separation would not desist until it claimed the very last one.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: draws far away, becomes distant
intimacy, the comfort of a close companion
ill omen, misfortune, bad luck
a paltry price, an insufficient compensation
separation, parting, the act of being torn apart

context: A brief elegy or farewell poem in the ṭawīl meter lamenting the imminent departure of a close friend. The poet declares that no price could compensate for the loss and that even a thousand souls would not satisfy separation's demand.

titleEn: Here Is the Station of the Grief-Stricken

verses.sadr: لو قيل ما كان مزورا بها
نوح صفا مذ عهد نوح له
نوح بن عمرو بن حوي بن عم
verses.ajuz: رو بن حوي بن الفتى ماتع

verses.sadr: إن يرفع السجف له اليوم ير
إن أنت لم تنهض به صاعدا

translationEn: Here is the station of the grief-stricken — it has become empty, the remnant of a devastating time. A dwelling that, after its dwellers, was watered by the turns of separation from their lethal poison. Blame not the lover — it is no novelty, this groaning of one pulled away. If it were said there was no visitor there, the ruined encampment would rejoice at the returner. Take heed and shed tears for a while — for tears are the twin of restraining grief. Its highlands were emptied by every slender, tall beauty who steals the heart of even the most kingly heart. One who is no weakling before the sword will wake in love for her, humble. Graceful — if you let your thought range through her beauty, it guides you to the Creator. Nūḥ — whose nobility has been pure since the time of Nūḥ, drinking glory from lofty lineage. His fathers unbroken in their lineage, like the dawn in its blazing radiance. Forebears you would think, from their brightness, to be the stations of the rising moon — like the Bucket, the Fish, the Pleiades, the Belly, the Star, all the way to the Swallower. Nūḥ ibn ʿAmr ibn Ḥawiyy ibn ʿAmr ibn Ḥawiyy ibn the youth Mātiʿ. His glory is that of the Saksak — Kindite glory — and the Adadī supremacy in its pure brightness. In drought his wealth is a pasture, and in plenty he satisfies the contented. In his tribe a lineage has shone brightly, elevated far above the scorching wind. How many a knight among them — when called to the rescue — like the glittering tip of a straight-shafted spear! The spear-shaft bends back or breaks, already quenched with flowing blood, from a wild thrust that tears through the armor of the fully mailed warrior. His decrees run through appointed fates — a command obeyed by the obedient. He clears for them the narrow pass on the day of battle, opening a gap in the ranks wide as a road. If Ḥawiyy fulfills my need, then grant it and calm the fearful, grief-stricken heart. A Yemeni youth like the Yemeni blade that sharpens its edges against the stopper. Shown in his glittering jewels, his eyelid, and in the keenness of his cutting sharp sword. He passes beyond ease and its shade toward night-travel and distant journey. More knowledgeable of the desert and better guided in it than the desert ant and the expert tracker. He knows that disease lurks beneath the calm of the magnificent horse. The flying omen in his affairs overrides the mark of the perching omen. He fails and pushes forward with ambition, leaving comfort to those who graze in contentment. His striving for glory is with a watchful eye, neither languid nor downcast. Bold action belongs only to the armored man who is full, or the noble man who hungers. I broadcast for him a fresh tale that the ear of the listener bends to hear. If the curtain is lifted for him today, tomorrow he will lift it in the brilliant assembly. Many an interceded-for man has not ceased striving until he became the intercessor for his intercessor. If you do not raise him upward in the radiant, fruitful aspiration until his hope is seen balanced after the stumbling of his limping wish — he who is counted upon as a provision has failed, and one who hopes in him is lost to loss.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the grief-stricken, one overwhelmed by distress
a tall, slender woman, elegant as a sword blade
a narrow pass or strait; a critical dangerous moment in battle
a small insect that lives in the desert; proverbially used for expert desert knowledge
a curtain, a veil; figuratively the barrier between obscurity and fame
a sudden bold strike; bold action taken with resolve
lurking, clinging closely beneath the surface

context: A long panegyric in the sarīʿ meter, opening with the conventional nasīb at the ruins before transitioning to praise of Nūḥ ibn ʿAmr, whose lineage Abu Tammam traces back through several generations of Kindite and Adadī nobility. The poem celebrates his martial valor and concludes with the poet's appeal for patronage.

titleEn: Your Servant Prays with Open Palms

verses.sadr: إن أنت لم تبك له رحمة
كم حسرة لي في الفؤاد الذي

verses.ajuz: في الناس لو حفوا به أنسه

translationEn: Your servant prays, palms outstretched, supplicating humbly — do not forget him. If you will not weep for him out of mercy, then do not blame him if he weeps for himself. How many a regret have I in the heart that I have long imprisoned in the dungeon of love. A servant — if you cause him desolation, you will not find his intimacy even among all the people gathered around him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: supplicating humbly, praying with earnest devotion
the five fingers; opening the hand in supplication
bitter regret, deep grief and longing
causing desolation, making someone feel utterly alone
intimacy, companionable warmth

context: A brief ghazal in the sarīʿ meter, cast as a servant's supplication to a beloved. The religious vocabulary of prayer is redeployed for erotic longing, a characteristic move in classical Arabic ghazal.

titleAr: أما الرسوم فقد أذكرن ما سلفا
titleEn: The Ruins Have Recalled What Passed

verses.sadr: أما الرسوم فقد أذكرن ما سلفا
verses.ajuz: فلا تكفن عن شأنيك أو يكفا

verses.sadr: لا عذر للصب أن يقنى الحياء ولا
verses.ajuz: للدمع بعد مضي الحي أن يقفا
في الربع يحسب من عينيه قد رعفا

verses.sadr: وفي الخدور مها لو أنها شعرت
verses.ajuz: إذا ظغت فرحا أو أبلست أسفا

verses.sadr: من كل خود دعاها البين فابتكرت
لا أظلم النأي قد كانت خلائقها
verses.ajuz: من ، قبل وشك النوى عندي نوى قذفا
بعذر من كان مشغوفا بها كلفا

verses.sadr: حتى لو ان الليالي صورت لغدت
إذا علا طود مجد ظل في نصب
verses.ajuz: أو يعتلي من سواه ذروة شعفا

verses.sadr: فلو تكلم خلق لا لسان له
قصد الخلائق إلا في وغى وندى
verses.ajuz: كلاهما سبة ما لم يكن سرفا

verses.sadr: تدعى عطاياه وفرا وهي إن شهرت
يقول قول الذي ليس الوفاء له

verses.ajuz: في ناظريه وإن كانا قد اختلفا

verses.sadr: ولو يقال اقر حد السيف شرهما
verses.ajuz: ما شام حديه حتى يقتل الخلفا
من اشتفى لهما من بابك وشفى

verses.sadr: في يوم أرشق والهيجاء قد رشقت

verses.ajuz: وكان في حلقات الرعب قد رسفا

verses.sadr: حيران يحسب سجف النقع من دهش
verses.ajuz: طودا يحاذر أن ينقض أو جرفا

verses.sadr: ظل القنا يستقي من صفه مهجا
من مشرق دمه في وجهه ، بطل ،
فذاك قد سقيت منه القنا جرعا
verses.ajuz: وذاك قد سقيت منه القنا نطفا

verses.sadr: ما إن رأيت سواما قبلها هملا
قد نبذوا الحجف المحبوك من زؤد
برق إذا برق غيث بات مختطفا
بالبيض قد أنفت إن الحسام إذا

verses.ajuz: وما خططت بها لاما ولا ألفا

verses.sadr: وغيضة الموت أعني البذ قدت لها

verses.ajuz: ما حولها الخيل حتى أصبحت طرفا

verses.sadr: أعطى بكلتا يديه حين قيل له
verses.ajuz: هذا أبو دلف العجلي قد دلفا
ذلا تمكن من عينيه ، لا وطفا

verses.sadr: يا رب مكرمة تجفى ، إذا نزلت
verses.ajuz: قد عرفت في ذراك البر واللطفا

verses.sadr: لو لم تفت مسن المجد مذ زمن
verses.ajuz: بالجود والبأس كان المجد قد خرفا

verses.sadr: نامت همومي عني حين قلت لها
verses.ajuz: حسبي أبو دلف ، حسبي به وكفى

translationEn: The ruins have recalled what passed — so do not cease your own affairs until they cease. The lovelorn has no excuse to put on modesty, nor do tears have excuse to stop after the tribe has departed — until the encampment lies awash in flowing water and blood from his eyes as if they bled. In the curtained quarters are gazelles — had they known, they would have departed in joy or lingered in sorrow. Pearls like bright stars, their skins clothed in the nacre of beauty, not mere shell. Every graceful one whom separation called — she departed a virgin, yet her abandonment became a half. I do not blame distance; her character before separation threatened to be a far-flung pit. Soft — the guardian of beauty was generous with her nature and fashioned her with his hands into a fresh meadow. Polished smooth, she concealed from us her breast — an innocent heart cooing at a gleaming eye. The blamer himself becomes enamored by his very censure of one besotted with her. Bid farewell to your heart as one bids farewell at parting — I do not see it returning from this journey of farewell. It wrestles with longing for a while, then that wrestling pulls it toward the rhymes in praise of Abū Dulaf. Through his generosity the days fell silent, wearing the bloom of youth when they had been old in nobility. So that if the nights were given form, his brilliant deeds would become earrings in their ears. When he climbs a mountain of glory, he strains or scales a pinnacle no other can reach. If a creature without a tongue could speak, the heights would call him to their fold as a marvel. He overflows with humility while the world, through his supremacy, nearly quakes from its edges with arrogance. Moderate in character except in battle and giving — both are shameful if not excessive. His gifts are called abundant, yet if made famous they would be a glory for those who take after him afresh. I never ceased awaiting a wonder to appear before us — until I saw Shuʾāl harvesting honor. He speaks like one for whom fulfillment is no resolve, yet carries out like one who has sworn an oath. He saw the dove as the sibling of promise and they united in his eyes even when they had differed. Both going and coming, devoted to his generosity and reconciled through his goodness. Were it said: let the sword's edge satisfy their thirst, it would not sheathe either edge until it had killed the successor. The caliph and al-Afshīn knew well who was avenged through Bābak and who healed them. On a day when Arshaq struck hard — and the battle had unleashed volleys from fate, heavy and shattering. Your person was a landmark in its inattentive moments and your judgment a torch of light in its darkness. You drew him out arrow-like from his quiver so that the prize of the outcome became his target. Through him you stretched the steps until they galloped, charging the clash when before they had moved slowly. Steps — you see the Indian sword vindicated through him, halving the reed-spear. You incited the army of right guidance and it pounced unsheathed, having been shackled in rings of terror. Bābak passed on, withered, stripped, his sweet blood — had it been sipped. Bewildered, mistaking the curtain of dust-clouds in his panic for a mountain fearing collapse or an eroding cliff. The lances kept drawing from his ranks souls — some in trickles, some in a gushing spring-flood. From one whose blood shone on his face — O warrior — and from one whose blood drained pale from terror. This one — the lances drank from him in gulps, that one — the lances drank from him in drops. Straightened spears that stripped the Byzantines of their blueness, the Arabs of their brownness, and the emaciated lover of his leanness. I have never seen grazing livestock, left to wander free, that would graze and deliver back to their own herd their leanness! Many a day like the days of battle — I left therein the shaft of the spear and the shaft of the horn, broken. I visited Ābarshtuʾ with lances aimed at the pit of death and the far-flung, impassable plain. When they saw you and your host assembled in mass — the brow of fate remained eclipsed before it. They fled and you covered them with a band of fierce chiefs rushing into death's depths, unconcealing, not retreating. They had cast off their woven shields in terror, and you made their heads — or rather you made them shields. You flashed the gleam of scabbards over their heads, striking with a crushing blow that makes the crooked forget his crookedness. A flash — when a rain-cloud flashes it is seized by the eye; it became a seizure for necks. The white swords grew too proud — for a sword when the midday blaze urges it for an hour grows too proud. You wrote on their faces with scoring and fine tracery — striking and thrusting that destroys skulls and the arrogant. Writing that is endlessly read — yet you drew in it neither lam nor alif. If they persist in denial, you have left their faces, by what you granted them, as open scrolls. The thicket of death — I mean al-Badhdhh — you drove toward it an overwhelming force trampling the rough ground. It had been the forbidden center, and the cavalry stripped all around it until it became the edge. Al-Afshīn remained wrapped in victory while Bābak lay covered in humiliation. He gave with both hands when it was said to him: This is Abū Dulaf al-ʿIjlī who has drawn near. You left his eyelids forever downcast — a humiliation that took hold in his eyes, not mere languor. O Lord, many a noble act scorned — when it descended upon you, it found goodness and gentleness in your shade. Had you not sharpened the whetstone of glory long ago with generosity and valor, glory would have grown senile. My cares fell asleep and ceased their assault when I said to them: Abū Dulaf is enough for me — he alone is enough and sufficient.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, traces of abandoned encampments
woven shields made of leather or wood
dust cloud raised in battle
a famous Abbasid general who defeated Bābak al-Khurramī in 837 CE
the fortified stronghold of Bābak in Azerbaijan
straightened spears; lances made perfectly straight by a craftsman
a light in darkness, a guiding brightness

context: One of Abu Tammam's great victory odes (futuḥiyyāt) in the basīṭ meter, celebrating the defeat of Bābak al-Khurramī and praising Abū Dulaf al-ʿIjlī. The poem opens with a conventional nasīb before turning to martial description of the campaign, culminating in the iconic image of the poet's cares put to sleep by the patron's sufficiency.

titleEn: Breath Urging Breath
translationEn: Breath urges breath, and tears that will not be held back. Encampments mantled for slumber, stripped of their former bond, worn away. They made public what I had kept hidden — speaking of love though they are mute.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: urges it on, hastens it forward
are held back, are restrained from flowing
encampments, dwelling places now abandoned
covered, mantled; also worn away and obliterated
mute, unable to speak — yet paradoxically they speak

context: An extremely brief lyric in the madīd meter — only three verses — condensing the entire emotional landscape of the nasīb: breathless sighing, uncontrollable tears, and ruined encampments that betray the secret of love through their very silence.

titleEn: Their Ruins Robbed of Graceful Gazelles

verses.ajuz: لا مطل في عدة ولا تسويفا

verses.sadr: من كل مطعمة الهوى جعلت لها
فإذا مشى يمشي الدفقى أو سرى
verses.ajuz: وصل السرى أو سار سار وجيفا

verses.sadr: كهل الأناة فتى الشذاة إذا غدا
وأخو الفعال إذا الفتى كل الفتى
كم من وساع الجود عندي في الندى
إن غاض ماء المزن فضت وإن قست
أنا ذو كساك محبة لا خلة
واف إذا الإحسان قنع لم يزل
هذا إلى قدم الذمام بك الذي

verses.ajuz: لك هضبة الحلم التي لو وازنت
إن كان بالورع ابتنى القوم العلى

translationEn: Their ruins were robbed of the slender graceful gazelles, exchanging them for wild beasts that lingered there. O dwelling that surrendered to events their due judgment — no delay in a promise and no deferral. Generosity cast anchor in your assembly-place, and the winds breathed faintly at your courtyard. The clouds fell passionately upon your open spaces, and at times watered your highlands — the wandering, infatuated one. If the guest of calamities lodges there, casting his burdens down, he has found a good host. Such is fate — its blows always frequent the dwelling of a familiar home. The years have left successors at your courtyard, though long the daughters of fate were absent from you. Days when no calamity struck your folk except that its turn reversed on itself, turned away. When events aimed at you with a glance, your gazelles turned back its gaze, dazzled. Every love-nurturing beauty — I made for her a portion of hearts' affections, offered. And the gentle companion of glances whose gentleness is followed by a fierce blow to the credulous heart. They traverse the attributes — trailing locks and cheeks, temples and eyes and noses. They were like the rising full moons, and they left for us settings of distance and eclipses. Gazelles of a tribe — a departure drained them and left you bleeding from the wine of separation. They had been the ornaments of their age, but then they split, and it was as if time had put on rough wool. The dull disposition of time became refined — what had been withheld turned neck-side and cheekbone. I bound myself to the generosity of Abū Saʿīd — through it my body of hope, which had been gaunt, grew full. I fortified myself with the lion whose roar has made the frontier passes into a dense thicket morning and evening. He combines roughness and gentleness in his own person, and so has become great in hearts and gentle. When he walks he walks with a brisk, sweeping stride, or when he journeys he journeys without pause, or when he travels he travels with a swift pace. Difficult affairs shook him and he shook them, and he was made to fear for God's sake and made others fear. Wakeful — experience has harvested his resolution into a sharp-eyed bundle and straightened his determination. He drew from his opinions sparks that, were they forged, would be swords. Deliberate as a man of maturity, brisk as a youth when he goes to war — he is the fierce eagle-vulture. A man of action — when a youth is fully a youth in valor and generosity, he stands as his successor. How many men of wide generosity in my experience of giving — when he ran and I ran, turned slow. You both did well in gifting me, but you were to me like spring in life while the other was autumn. Both of you have mounted the heights — and you rode them at the very summit while the other came as a rider behind. If the raincloud's water dried up, you poured forth, and if the times grew hard against me, you were compassionate. When their characters failed or became barren, you set about smoothing for me fertile character. And gifts sought after and attained, leaving the noble man, through their bounty, nobly elevated. They suffice for the first and second draft of affliction's thirst at the moment of asking — wrestling and deadly. Listen — a green, fresh flourishing blessing has settled in your dwelling, gleaming with luster. Lush — when blessings depart it pitches its tent and when they flee it becomes to you a devoted familiar. I am one who clothed you in love, not mere friendship — the ink of odes woven in brocade pattern. A refiner who adorned you with arrangements of wonders that became earrings for the ears of kings. Faithful — when goodness is content, the face of the benefaction before me remains ever uncovered. And when a kindness is forgotten, the kindness of your hand among them becomes known as a kindness. This in addition to the ancient bond you have with me — had it been born a child it would be a grown attendant. And an inner self consumed by sincerity and love — had it been a season it would be a summer. And a breast's resting place in you whose alarm persists — were it a frontier pass it would be a feared one. If I have lengthened my praise it is for gifts of yours that are neither bounded nor described. You have lowered fate's pressure from me after a blow that left the grinding of its fangs upon me. The benefit of one deeply rooted in knowledge — that he will spend his life: the grandeur of religion, morose in generosity, negating strength while affirming obligation. I will say a counselor's sincere word that leans toward a pure heart, clean in your pleasure. You have a plateau of forbearance that, were it weighed against Ajāʾ when it grew heavy, would be light. And a sweetness of character that, were it mixed with the dull disposition of time, would become refined. I see you campaigning in the land of enemies — without respite, dry and shriveled. If the people built glory through piety, or if the noble man became noble through fear of God — then why was Zānn ʿĀmir elevated while ʿAlqama was dismissed though he was chaste? And Ḥātim built noble deeds in his polytheism while another demolishes them though he professes the faith?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slender-waisted, graceful — describing the gazelles that have abandoned the ruins
white gazelles; a standard poetic image for beautiful women
dense thicket of reeds and trees, a jungle
a brisk, sweeping, rapid manner of walking
the straightening of a spear shaft; by extension the sharpening of resolve
earrings; figuratively adornments of great value
a bond of obligation, an ancient covenant of loyalty

context: A long panegyric in the kāmil meter praising Abū Saʿīd, comparing him favorably to other patrons. The poem ends controversially with the argument that piety alone does not create nobility — Ḥātim al-Ṭāʾī built noble deeds as a pagan while some believers destroy them, a bold theological provocation characteristic of Abu Tammam.

titleEn: He Stole a Glance in Bewilderment
translationEn: He stole a glance — perhaps bewildered — gazing from a shy, contracted eye. He shot my heart with his glance; the arrow of his eyes did not miss. The hand of beauty has engraved on his cheeks the most exquisite engraving. My thirst is quenched only by his kiss — so when shall I be satisfied from this thirst?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stole surreptitiously, glanced furtively
contracted, shy, recoiling — describing a timid, furtive eye
did not miss the target, hit precisely
beauty, elegance, grace
satiation from thirst, quenching

context: A brief and witty ghazal in the mujzaʾ al-kāmil meter. The poem builds on the classic arrow-of-glance metaphor and ends on a paradox: the lover's thirst can only be quenched by a kiss, but such quenching is never attained.

titleEn: Tell Ibrahim and Al-Fadl

verses.sadr: وعلمت ما لقي المزور إذا همت

translationEn: Say to Ibrāhīm and al-Faḍl — whose affection settled in the southern recesses of my heart — that towering clouds with high humps and heavy shoulders prevented visit and reunion. They wronged the people of the pressing pilgrimage, yet did justice to the wide expanse of the earth — what justice! They brought the benefit of the meadows and their harm to the people of the dwellings, the tongues of the describers. And I learned what a visitor suffers when they pour down with foul-smelling rain and light mud. So I was cold to you — and I learned in such situations that the connection itself becomes a harsh cutting off. When the cloud stood with its full, heavy teats, gathered from all its flanks and sides. All the rich waterways testified that it was from a raincloud noble in all its edges. Its births in one land are not exhausted until a mating camel is made ready for another. How many gifts the green earth carried in its loads to the soil — treasures and fine presents. I can already picture the meadow unveiled for it, displaying a robe from its brocade of fragrance. Abundant fruiting, and a complete growth of the valley-floor, and flowers that crackle like caldrons in boiling. And I can picture the departing travelers — a stopping place for which friends weep to friends. And I can picture the wide-jawed she-camels in their midst, with green cheeks, thick legs, and light hooves. Winter, for all the gloom of its face, is truly the benefactor whose gift is the openness of summer. As if its traces from the raincloud are upon the soft-sloped grounds and hollows and mixed-color sands. Traces of the hands of the house of Muṣʿab, extended without blame or breaking of promise. It is obligatory upon you, when you settle in their encampments, not to see them as one leaving behind a petitioner. They are, in their goodness and their solicitude for the seeker, as if guests were themselves the hosts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the pericardium, the membrane around the heart; the deepest inner recess
rain-clouds, the clouds that carry rain
udders of a camel; here metaphorically the belly of the rain-cloud
a famous breed of wide-jawed, noble she-camels
mixed-colored sands; also men of different mothers
the shank of an animal, the lower leg of a camel

context: A panegyric in the kāmil meter addressed to Ibrāhīm and al-Faḍl from the house of Muṣʿab. Abu Tammam excuses his absence through rain and mud, then uses the rain-imagery to transition into praise of the patrons' generosity as natural abundance flowing to all who seek it.

titleEn: I Endured with Unconquered Patience
translationEn: I endured separation from you with patience not conquered — yet with tears streaming upon my cheeks. You have made me a resting place for passion, a homeland for grief, O resting place of beauty and goodness. If I were to deny what I have suffered through loving you, the witnesses of my torments and my torture would testify against me truthfully.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: poured out, flowing freely — of tears
a resting place, an abode, a settled dwelling
torments, intense pains of longing and love-grief
denied you, refused to acknowledge (what I experienced)
torture, prolonged suffering — here the beloved's cruelty

context: A brief but compressed ghazal in the basīṭ meter, built on the paradox of patient endurance betrayed by flowing tears. The address to the beloved as 'resting place of beauty and goodness' contrasts sharply with the poet's own state as a resting place of grief.

titleEn: By Him Who Gave You Strength and Power

verses.ajuz: علي وأزرى بي وضعف من بطشي

verses.sadr: لقد خلق الله الهوى لك خالصا
verses.ajuz: ومكنه في الصدر مني بلا غش

verses.sadr: سل الليل عني هل أذوق رقاده
عناء بمن لو قال للشمس أاقبلي
verses.ajuz: للبته أو جاءت على رغمها تمشي

verses.sadr: قضيب من الريحان في غير لونه
verses.ajuz: وأم رشا في غير اكراعها الخمش

translationEn: By Him who gave you strength and power over me, diminishing me and weakening my own strength — God created passion purely for you and lodged it in my breast without any taint. Ask the night about me — do I taste its slumber, and do my ribs find rest upon my bed? A toil for someone who, if he said to the sun: come near! — it would come at his bidding or walk toward him despite itself. A branch of sweet basil but not of its color, and a mother of a fawn but not of its slender scratched legs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: overpowering force, violent strength of grip
diminished, looked down upon, weakened
ribs; the seat of inner emotion
sweet basil; used as a metaphor for a slender, fragrant beloved
a doe, a female gazelle — a standard image of feminine beauty

context: A short ghazal in the ṭawīl meter, opening with an oath invoking God's power and ending with two elaborate similes describing the beloved. The final verse is typical of Abu Tammam's style: combining two conventional images with a dissonant detail that complicates the comparison.

titleEn: A Sick Man Wept at Sick Ruins

verses.sadr: أرج أقام من الأحبة في الثرى
وحدي وقفت ولم أقل من عبرة
وحسدت ما غادرت فيها من بلى
حتى إذا تمت فلم يعجزه من
من كان يقصد في نصيحته لها
لم يبلغ القلم الذي يجدي به

translationEn: A lovesick man wept at the signs of a lovesick encampment — had it not been for the breeze of its soil, it would not have been recognized. Good were the feet that trod its soil — they sent forth the fragrance of a perfume bag mingled with cold water. A fragrance settled from the beloved ones in the dust, and standing water poured out by the streaming tears. Decay seized its signs and threw them with the hand of scorching winds into the faces of the white poplars. I stood alone — my sobs would not stop pouring — my inner self stood there, saying to our camel-driver: halt. I envied what remained of its ruins, and I tested them with the flash of a grief-darkened glance. I kept pressing insistently in questioning its traces — yet the denial is from the grace of the one who asks insistently. Let its grief settle in the heart — a consuming kernel of longing — along with what is felt toward those who departed and those who remained. As if Muḥammad had prayed for rain for them — and their traces are from the blessing in embellishment. He asked the Spica star and it gave generously from its modesty — a heavy rain with a glittering, heavy-lashed cloud. The interlocked flowers spreading as the east wind unfolds them, moist, and folds them like the folding of a silk garment. Spring has taken up residence there and no hot-breathed summer scorching wind can lift it. I brought my hopes to you on a garden-bred she-camel, powerful, never mated with an inferior stallion. Born fully — she encompassed a hundred and settled in half again and went beyond the increment. She came to my dwelling — she a womb to her daughters — travelling on the two feet of a swirling, cold wind. An expert in her stallions selected her — knowing her created nature intimately, gentle. When she was complete and nothing of her reserved limbs escaped the eager one. She came to me with a chest full of energy, forefeet that flutter and a turning hindquarter. She glides through waves resembling their crests in the manner of the praised one in an unjust time. Then she sheltered my carcass and I became her unborn young, secure in the settled darkness of her belly. When she stumbles with her companions she recalls it — and pieces of a dark night pass beneath me. Her labor brought her after its stages to a youth approaching manhood, slender of body like a mature man of middle age. Crooked — she snatches the reins and treads upon crookedness — she excels in snatching the trailing garment. I gestured with the slow folding of a thing in her humps — and she swooped like the wary serpent of the hard rock. She came to you while the devil was scattering her shadow, and came while surpassing even al-Aḥnaf's forbearance. Whoever was moderate in sincerity toward her — Muḥammad in sincerity is the very essence of excess. You struck fire from my flint-stones in compassion and gleaming — so they aimed at the departing, pursuing one. Those who obtained destruction and those who gathered wealth through Muḥammad — at the caliph's court are sinners and petitioners. In God he fulfills his promise and his threat to the petitioners and to the stubborn, indulgent one. The bowels of the subjects settled in the bowels of a keen heart, via a sharp tongue. The pen that serves him in God's cause has not reached a thousand — sharpened and straightened. In the hands of the spiritual substitutes when they aimed with it — gathered — they worked by what is in the Book. He draws out the treacherous eyes with a glance that encompasses their hidden thoughts before they even blink.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gravely ill from love; the lovesick one
a bag of musk or perfume, carried on a camel
hot scorching winds of summer
a spring flower, yellow ranunculus, used for meadow imagery
the number one hundred; a poetic way of saying a hundred
spiritual substitutes, holy men who maintain the world through their piety
the Quran, the written scripture

context: A long panegyric in the kāmil meter addressed to Muḥammad, the Caliph or a high official. The poem features an extended allegory of the poet's ode as a she-camel bred through generations of poetic craft, culminating in praise of the patron's role as a spiritual and political pillar whose justice and piety sustain the community.

titleEn: Your Sincere Servant Answers Your Call
translationEn: Your servant answers your call sincerely, weeping blood as many times as the pebbles. A servant whose heart obeyed you — the obedient one is not like the disobedient. Your beauty inflamed the infatuated one until he was filled and particularly singled out. He tried to free himself from loving you but could not manage to be free.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: answered the call, responded to a summons — from the pilgrimage formula
sickness, infatuation; one who is sick with love
was particularly singled out, especially afflicted
freeing oneself, extricating oneself from a situation
a servant, a slave — the lover's self-abasement before the beloved

context: A brief ghazal in the mujzaʾ al-kāmil meter. The opening word 'labbāk' (answering your call) deliberately echoes the pilgrimage formula, casting the lover's submission in a quasi-religious register typical of Abu Tammam's sophisticated wordplay.

titleAr: أغنيت عني غناء الماء في الشرق
titleEn: You Enriched Me as Water Enriches the Thirsty

verses.sadr: أغنيت عني غناء الماء في الشرق
لو كان خيم أبي يعقوب في حجر
ما من جميل من الدنبا ولا حسن
يا منة لك لولا ما أخففها
verses.ajuz: به من الشكر لم تحمل ولم تطق

translationEn: You enriched me the way water enriches the parched land, and you were the one who kindled the heavy rain of the rising cloud. You renewed for me a hope whose pasturing grounds had been stalled before it in a worn-out quest. Had Abū Yaʿqūb's tent been pitched on barren rock, it would have overflowed with water gushing from him. There is no beauty in this world nor any loveliness except that most of it resides in that character. What a favor from you — had it not been lightened by the gratitude I offer, it could not have been carried or endured. By God, repel from me the due of its crushing weight, for I fear for my neck under it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: extreme thirst; the parched state of one dying of thirst
the broadside rain-cloud, abundant and wide
its grazing livestock, its pasturing hopes
a tent; also an innate disposition or character
a favor of such weight it creates obligation; a gift that burdens the recipient

context: A short poem of gratitude in the basīṭ meter to Abū Yaʿqūb. Abu Tammam uses the paradox of an obligation so heavy it threatens to break the neck of the grateful, and concludes with a witty appeal to the patron to relieve him of the burden his own generosity has created.

titleAr: لي لا كان من هواك خلاص
titleEn: There Is No Escape from Your Love

verses.sadr: لي لا كان من هواك خلاص

translationEn: For me there is no escape from your love — and may my body suffer, not you, the diminishment. Here is the harm done to me, and here is my heart — melt it as lead is melted. Why did I turn away when I secretly hunted a glance from you — and you are the hunter for me!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: escape, release, freeing oneself
diminishment, reduction, being brought low
lead — used for the image of melting down from love-grief
hunted stealthily, tried to capture covertly
a hunter; one who traps others

context: A very short ghazal in the khafīf meter, ending with a self-accusatory wit: the poet stole a glance from the beloved but is himself the prey, not the hunter.

titleAr: قد شرد الصبح هذا الليل عن أفقه
titleEn: Dawn Has Chased Night from the Horizon

verses.sadr: قد شرد الصبح هذا الليل عن أفقه
verses.ajuz: وسوغ الدهر ما قد كان من شرقه

verses.sadr: سيقت إلى الخلق في النيروز عافية
verses.ajuz: بها شفاهم جديد الدهر من خلقه

verses.sadr: لما اكتسى القاسم البرد الأنيق غدا
الله عافاه من كرب ومن وصب
verses.ajuz: كاد السماح يذوق الموت من فرقه

verses.sadr: لم يبق ذو كرم إلا وجامعة
verses.ajuz: ثقيلة قد حناها الدهر في عنقه
رب كساك الأثيث النضر من ورقه

verses.sadr: حتى يقال لقد أضحى أبو دلف
verses.ajuz: وخلقه قد طغى حسنا على خلقه

translationEn: Dawn has chased this night from its horizon, and fate has allowed what had been its state of parching thirst. Well-being was driven to all creatures on Nawrūz — through it the new era healed them of what fate had wrought. O Lord, many a one who began the day in grief and ended it in grief — who recovered, and who lay entangled at night and found rest. When al-Qāsim clothed himself in the elegant robe, he went toward joy and gave joy's infection to his burning. God healed him from distress and affliction — generosity nearly tasted death from the fear of losing him. No generous man remained without a heavy fetter that fate had bent upon his neck. He brought you the ripest of the fruits of piety — a Lord who clothed you in the lush fresh green of His foliage — until it is said: Indeed Abū Dulaf has become such that his noble character has overwhelmed his bodily beauty in excellence.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: choking on water, extreme thirst; gasping
Nawrūz, the Persian new year; a major Abbasid court celebration
one who takes the morning drink; one who begins the day in a certain state
a neck-fetter, a shackle placed on the neck
noble character and moral virtue — contrasted here with physical appearance

context: A Nawrūz (New Year) poem in the basīṭ meter praising al-Qāsim, likely al-Qāsim ibn ʿĪsā, and addressing Abū Dulaf. The poem celebrates the new year as a renewal of well-being and praises the patron's character as surpassing even his physical beauty.

titleEn: He Who Robs My Eyes of Sleep
translationEn: He who robs my eyes of the pleasure of sleep, and makes one part of me weep for another part. He who kills me unjustly with his turning away, and with his glance that averts its eyes downward. Beware — do not oppress one in need upon whom you inflict what you decree. One who envies the earth out of his anxious care for the spot where your sandals touch the ground.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the closing of the eyes in sleep, slumber
turning away, averting the face in disdain
one who averts and lowers the gaze
dire need, poverty, neediness — here the need of the lover
a place of treading, the ground one walks upon

context: A short ghazal in the sarīʿ meter. The final verse is among Abu Tammam's most celebrated conceits of hyperbolic love: the poet envies the very earth for the privilege of bearing the beloved's footstep.

titleEn: Fate's Turns Come from Your Separation

verses.sadr: ما السبق إلا سبق يحاز على
verses.ajuz: جواد قوم لم يجر في طلقك

verses.sadr: اقبض يدا عن أبي الحسين تجد

translationEn: The turns of fate were brought about by your separation — and the destitute took shelter in your foliage. Precedence is only precedence seized upon a noble steed of a people that has not run in your open course. O fate, set right your two neck-sides — you have deafened these people with your recklessness. Ask your nights — they know well which generous man you have shackled in your fetter. Withdraw your hand from Abū al-Ḥusayn and you will find his new generosity returning upon your own character. How much anguish is there for generosity, and how much restlessness for glory and noble deeds in your restlessness! May God clothe you in a garment of well-being in your troubled sleep and in your wakefulness. May illness leave your body as you have driven blame from deeds from your neck. May it pour upon you until your character in it is seen as sounder than your bodily constitution.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the turns and vicissitudes of fate
the jugular vein; also the side of the neck — 'set right your two sides'
recklessness, blundering incompetence
shackled, put in chains (plural feminine, referring to the nights)
burning anguish, the searing pain of grief

context: A poem in the munṣarih meter addressing fate directly and praising Abū al-Ḥusayn. Abu Tammam paradoxically tells fate to withdraw its hand from the patron — implying that the patron's own generosity will outlast fate's power — and closes with a prayer for the patron's health.

titleEn: Perfumed with Musk on His Cheeks
translationEn: Perfumed with musk upon his cheeks — beautiful of nature and sorcerous of speech. You always see traces on his cheeks from what the glances cause to run upon them. You see him all his life smiling — yet when he sees me he passes by as if enraged. In my heart and my ribs and my inward parts from love of him is a heat like the heat of a flame.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: perfumed, anointed heavily with scent
character traits, natural dispositions; also physical graces
glances from the corner of the eye
one who is enraged, furious — the beloved's coldness toward the poet
a tongue of flame, a fierce jet of fire

context: A brief ghazal in the kāmil meter describing a beautiful beloved who smiles at everyone except the poet, who is left burning with inner fire. The contrast between the beloved's perpetual smile and his coldness specifically toward the poet captures the essential pathos of unrequited love.

titleEn: O Lightning Visiting the Abraq Abode

verses.sadr: لا شوق ما لم تصل وجدا بالتي
يغلي إذا لم يضطرم ويرى إذا
verses.ajuz: لم يحتدم ، ويغص إن لم يشرق
من فارة المسك التي لم تفتق

verses.sadr: صلتان يبسط إن ردى أو إن عدا
verses.ajuz: في الأرض باعا منه ليس بضيق

verses.sadr: مسود شطر مثل ما اسود الدجى

verses.ajuz: في صهوتيه العين لم تتعل ق

verses.sadr: في مطلب أو مهرب أو رغبة
verses.ajuz: أو رهبة أو موكب أو فيلق
داني ثرى اليد من رجاء المملق

verses.sadr: مجلي قتام الوجه يذهل إن بدا
verses.ajuz: لك في الندي عن الشباب المونق

verses.sadr: لو كان سيفا ما استبت لنصله
لم يتبع شنع اللغات ولا مشى
يجني جناة النحل من أعلى الربا
أنف البلاغة لا كمن هو حائر
تنشق في ظلم المعاني إن دجت
واقرب إليه فإن أحرى المزن أن
verses.ajuz: يروي الثرى ما كان غير محلق

translationEn: O lightning visiting a dwelling at al-Abraq, driving the clouds with the chanting of she-camels! Ruins that bent the heart's resolve and tore the tears of the eye in every direction. There is no true longing unless you join an ecstasy with one who refuses your union like fire refusing the kindling. It boils when not yet blazing, and is seen when not yet intensely burning, and chokes when it does not flame. Despite the deprivation it refuses any attainment — unless it be pure water diluted. Scant — like a stray wafting forced from a musk-bag still unopened. What a noble horse prancing proudly in its tether-ropes, filled with haughtiness and strutting! With hooves that have dug and a spine that is hard, and long hair at the fetlocks and well-formed chest. With a blaze of white markings — the little of it upon its two haunches like the first white hairs of a parting. One with a touch of madness under the dust — and that madness is only from the excess of his health. Eyes are drawn to him, and a poet splits open in describing him spontaneously though he is not naturally a splitter of verses. With what ascends from his beauty and what descends, and what converges in his form and what diverges. Sleek — he stretches when he trots or runs a reach upon the ground that is not narrow. The spirited wildness of running appears in him when he gallops, and the haughtiness is in him without beating. His ancestor Kanāz guided him in the past to his like and chose his father to refine him. Half of him dark as the darkness of night, half of him white as the whiteness of a smooth parchment. The white markings flowed over him like a valley-floor flood — some spreading over him and some meeting. As if his rider, when he appears at full stretch, is the piebald son of al-Ṣabāḥ. Smooth of skin as if he had been clothed in a mantle of silk and brocade. Smooth and sleek — had the eye hung upon his two haunches it would not slip. He mounts though not a bitten snake, and he goes armed without weapons — the armor of an awe-inspiring man who has nothing. Whether in pursuit or flight, desire or fear, a procession or a battle-formation. Al-Ḥasan ibn Wahb gifted him this horse — he is one whose hand's soil is near to the hope of the destitute. The overflow of his right hand is counted with the rain-stars, and is reckoned among the good deeds of the people of the East. He draws down distant hope with his radiant face — the radiance of a thicket at the coming of lush spring. Clouds rarely call their seekers to their bounty unless they flash lightning. A dispeller of dark gloom of face — when he appears at the assembly he makes you forget the captivating youth in wonder. Were he a sword, the back of his blade would not submit itself for its extreme brilliance and lustre. Firm in eloquence when a speaker is bewildered — one who has become a hobble to a free tongue. He has not followed the ugliness of dialects nor walked the shackled walk of one fettered in the limits of grammar. In this way he divides speech, and this is like a wall struck for it and a moat. He gathers the harvest of bees from the hilltops, flowers, and dips into the brimming pool. The pride of eloquence — not like one who wanders confused, wavering in a withered, dry place. A herd of wild asses that scatters when another drives them — but when he drives them gently they gather. In the darknesses of meanings when they grow dense, from him break the heralds of illumined speech. Clothe Sulaymān in wealth and open for him a gate facing ease that is not locked. Draw near to him — for the most worthy cloud to water the earth is one that does not fly too high. His intercession is of proven worth — what value has a sharp Yemeni sword if it is not freed from the scabbard? And his equipment is noted — how many a hidden need has grown in the folds of a fine garment of the elegant wearer. A troublesome matter concealed between the smooth-gaited mount and the folded cloak.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place-name; also land with black and white stripes of rock and soil
a touch of madness, a wildness of spirit — here applied to the horse
white markings, bright patches on a horse's coat
the watered pattern on a sword blade; its brilliance
the shackled gait of a prisoner in chains — used here of a constrained speaker
a horse with a smooth, ambling gait

context: A panegyric in the kāmil meter with an extended horse description (waṣf al-faras) as the central set-piece, the horse being a gift from al-Ḥasan ibn Wahb. The poem praises al-Ḥasan's eloquence and generosity and concludes with an intercession on behalf of Sulaymān. Horse description poems were a major genre in Abbasid court poetry.

titleEn: My Father Ransoms Him, Beautiful as He Is

verses.ajuz: من ليس يعرف غير ما أربي

translationEn: May my father ransom him — beautiful though that price is — this one who knows nothing but what pleases me. I hit the mark ten times over in his affection, in its like from the speed of my pursuit. And I see myself — had I stopped my hand for two months shooting at the ground, I would not have hit it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may my father be your ransom — an expression of great affection
what pleases me, what gives me advantage and delight
hit the target, struck the mark — from shooting
I would not have hit the target — I would have been unable
deep affection, genuine love and attachment

context: A very brief ghazal in the kāmil meter. The poem employs the archery metaphor: the poet hit the mark in winning the beloved's affection, but outside of that love his aim has become worthless — a hyperbolic confession that love itself has defined all his purpose.

titleEn: Grant My Eyes a Share of Sleep

verses.ajuz: من قبل أن تسمع لي لفظا

translationEn: Grant my eyes a share of slumber, and do not be a harsh master over me. Have my eyes no sanctity with you, since they have labored in gazing upon your beauty? You assigned me guilt and punished me before hearing a word from me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slumber, drowsiness, sleep
harsh, rough, brutal — describing a hard master
sanctity, inviolable respect, the right to protection
gazing from the corner of the eye, the side-glance
a fault, a sin, a charge laid against someone

context: A short ghazal in the sarīʿ meter, in which the poet pleads with the beloved for sleep, using the rhetoric of justice: the beloved punished the poet before hearing his defense, making the complaint at once amorous and humorously legalistic.

titleEn: Let Me Pour My Tears for You
translationEn: Leave me — streaming tears from my eyes and the rushing of your overflowing grief. And your threatening me with a remoteness that has come and lengthened — for far from the goal is the lot of the freed ones. And closeness — you are that — so if they both strike me in tangled conflict and in elevation. Young she-camels — no edge of my worry guards them, nor does my sword guard them on the morning of anxiety. When the journey asks them for effort, they pour for us the bucket of the soft trot to the water-troughs. Their lean return weighs lightly on me when I come back with fulfilled hopes shining bright. A greeting that makes the inner parts tremble — upon al-Ḥasan ibn Wahb and upon Iraq. Upon the beloved land to me — the lowlands and the highlands — and the youth of sweet acquaintance. We incline toward his gentle plains-like character — little rocky ground and hard stone among them. And can a gathering catastrophe that has collapsed upon those characters have any portion? Nights when we were in the drowsy ease of life, as if fate from them was in a shackle. And days belonging to us and to him — tender to us — we were stripped of their fine edges. Passion was our occupation — closeness and proximity — and the cup-bearer poured for us with the cup of longing. As if the covenant were from deer among us, though the meeting was a true meeting. I will water the caravan from his memory — pure and mixed — from the enduring words. A drink whose greatness in drinking is the drinking itself, and the rest is relief for the companions. And rhymes are ever refreshed between us — swift to be lost from being caught. When they are tethered they trot — yet they are not, when freed, creatures of free galloping. Upon their flanks and sides are perfumed loads of praise and yearning. Doubled in longing — the trace of separation clearly visible upon their surfaces.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the inner corners of the eyes where tears gather
young she-camels in their prime; here a metaphor for the poet's verses
a smooth, flowing trot of a camel — a comfortable travelling pace
perfume loads carried on camels; bags of precious scent
intense yearning, the last drops of longing in a vessel
tender, soft; also a place-name, a land of gentle terrain

context: A poem in the wāfir meter combining nasīb-like grief at separation with a long poetic farewell to al-Ḥasan ibn Wahb and Iraq. Abu Tammam describes his odes as she-camels loaded with perfume and longing, and pledges to preserve the memory of his patron and the beloved land in verse for the traveling companions.

titleEn: Beauty of Wondrous Form Smiling Like the Moon

verses.sadr: كل ما منظر رأيت من الحس

translationEn: A wondrous beauty laughing with his lights — like the full moon at the beginning of its rising. Every eye of elegance that beheld him returned from him with a wondrous beauty. Every beautiful sight you have seen of loveliness — all of it is entirely found in him. Except that the eyes harvest with the hands of their glance from his two cheeks the flowers of spring.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wondrous, unprecedented, of rare and astonishing beauty
beheld him, unveiled him to the gaze
elegance, the beautiful composure of a refined gazer
the two cheeks; often the site of the beloved's bloom and color
the flowers of spring; used for the bloom of the beloved's cheeks

context: A brief ghazal in the khafīf meter, a compressed meditation on all-encompassing beauty. The final verse captures Abu Tammam's characteristic blend of sensory delight and intellectual wit: the eyes do not merely look but actively harvest blossoms from the beloved's cheeks.
